title: Naming unrelated words predicts creativity (the Divergent Association Task)
authors: Jay A. Olson, Johnny Nahas, Denis Chmoulevitch, Simon J. Cropper, Margaret E. Webb
venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)
finding: Asking people to name 10 maximally-unrelated nouns and scoring the mean pairwise GloVe cosine distance (the DAT) correlates with established creativity tasks (AUT, etc.) at least as strongly as those tasks correlate with each other, with good test-retest reliability — and the scoring is fully automatic, sample-independent, and requires no human or LLM judge.
how_to_use: This is the canonical example of an HONEST objective divergence score: the metric is a fixed embedding-distance function with no grader in the loop, so it cannot be gamed by a model judging its own output. Use the DAT protocol (mean pairwise distance over a fixed-length set, deduplicated, valid dictionary words only) as the reference design for a no-judge divergence benchmark, and cite its validity ceiling — semantic distance only ever matched, never beat, human raters.
verified_title: Naming unrelated words predicts creativity
verification_note: Confirmed via Semantic Scholar: title 'Naming unrelated words predicts creativity', PNAS, 2021, DOI 10.1073/pnas.2022340118. Close match.
venue_verified: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

title: Semantic Distance and the Alternate Uses Task: Recommendations for Reliable Automated Assessment of Originality
authors: Roger E. Beaty, Dan R. Johnson, Daniel C. Zeitlen, Boris Forthmann
arxiv_id: N/A (Creativity Research Journal, not on arXiv)
finding: Raw semantic-distance originality scores are confounded by response length/elaboration and by fluency (idea count): longer or more numerous responses mechanically inflate distance. They show the confound is attenuated by removing stop words, by item-level choices (avoid compound-word prompts, use many items), and by explicitly modeling/partialling out elaboration and fluency before interpreting distance as originality.
how_to_use: This is the methodological backbone of an honest benchmark: it names the exact ways a distance metric LIES (verbosity and quantity masquerading as novelty) and gives the fixes — stop-word stripping, length normalization/covariate control, and per-item rather than aggregate scoring. Any divergence benchmark must report scores both raw and length-controlled, or it is measuring word count.
verified_title: Semantic Distance and the Alternate Uses Task: Recommendations for Reliable Automated Assessment of Originality
verification_note: Confirmed via Crossref: exact title match, Creativity Research Journal, issue 3, published 2022-01-24. Exact match.

title: Automating creativity assessment with SemDis: An open platform for computing semantic distance
authors: Roger E. Beaty, Dan R. Johnson
arxiv_id: N/A (Behavior Research Methods, not on arXiv)
finding: No single semantic space is canonical: a composite that averages cosine distances across multiple embedding models (e.g., several GloVe/word2vec/LSA spaces) predicts human originality ratings more reliably than any individual model, because idiosyncratic errors of one space wash out in the mean.
how_to_use: Guards against cherry-picking the embedding model that flatters your system. An honest benchmark should pre-register a fixed ensemble of public embedding spaces and report the composite, not the single best-correlating space — and should freeze model versions so results are reproducible rather than drifting with a hosted API.
verified_title: Automating creativity assessment with SemDis: An open platform for computing semantic distance
verification_note: Confirmed via Crossref: exact title match, Behavior Research Methods, online 2020-08-31 (vol 53, 2021). Exact match.

title: Beyond semantic distance: Automated scoring of divergent thinking greatly improves with large language models (Ocsai)
authors: Peter Organisciak, Selcuk Acar, Denis Dumas, Kelly Berthiaume
arxiv_id: N/A (Thinking Skills and Creativity; preprint ERIC ED629879)
finding: Fine-tuning LLMs on human-judged AUT responses (Ocsai) reaches r up to ~.81 with human raters — near the human inter-rater ceiling and far above bag-of-words semantic distance — because distance models treat a response as independent words and ignore context/compositionality. But the model must be supervised on human judgments per task; it is a calibrated predictor of human ratings, not an independent oracle.
how_to_use: Defines the honest ceiling and the honest danger. Honest use: a frozen, human-supervised scorer can be a second axis (rated originality) alongside no-judge distance — but only if it was trained on humans for THIS task and never on the candidate systems' own outputs. Dishonest use (avoid): letting a general LLM free-grade outputs, which is grading-your-own-homework and inherits the model's own stylistic preferences.
verified_title: Beyond semantic distance: Automated scoring of divergent thinking greatly improves with large language models
verification_note: Confirmed via Crossref: exact title match, Thinking Skills and Creativity, published 2023-09. Exact match.

title: We're Different, We're the Same: Creative Homogeneity Across LLMs
authors: Emily Wenger, Yoed N. Kenett
finding: On standardized creativity tests, individual LLM responses can score as high-originality as humans, yet population-level diversity collapses: LLM responses are far more similar to OTHER LLMs' responses than humans are to each other, even after controlling for response structure. Per-item originality and population diversity are different quantities, and per-item scores hide the collapse.
how_to_use: The single most important honesty correction for this axis: a system can win on mean per-response distance while producing a narrow, repetitive distribution. An honest 'thinks outside the box' benchmark must report a population/diversity metric (e.g., mean pairwise distance ACROSS many sampled outputs, distinct-n, coverage of the response space) — not just mean originality of one output — and compare against a human distribution as the reference.
verified_title: We're Different, We're the Same: Creative Homogeneity Across LLMs
verification_note: Confirmed via arXiv: exact title match, authors Emily Wenger and Yoed Kenett. arxiv_id correct.

title: Magic, Madness, Heaven, Sin: LLM Output Diversity is Everything, Everywhere, All at Once
authors: Harnoor Dhingra
finding: Argues output diversity is not a single model-intrinsic number: it is task- and objective-relative (epistemic/factual, interactional/utility, societal/representation, safety/robustness), and optimizing one objective (e.g., safety) can suppress creative diversity. A lone 'diversity score' is therefore underspecified and can mislead.
how_to_use: Forces the benchmark to state WHICH diversity it claims and to keep quality and diversity as separate, jointly-reported axes rather than one headline number — so a system can't trade away correctness or coherence to inflate a diversity score and call it 'creativity'. Pair every divergence number with a validity/appropriateness check.
verified_title: Magic, Madness, Heaven, Sin: LLM Output Diversity is Everything, Everywhere, All at Once
verification_note: Confirmed via arXiv: exact title match, author Harnoor Dhingra. arxiv_id 2604.01504 correct.

title: NoveltyBench: Evaluating Language Models for Humanlike Diversity
authors: Yiming Zhang, Harshita Diddee, Susan Holm, Hanchen Liu, Xinyue Liu, Vinay Samuel, Barry Wang, Daphne Ippolito
finding: Defines diversity at the SEMANTIC (functional-equivalence) level, not the lexical level: k generations are partitioned into equivalence classes by a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3-large classifier (trained on 1,000 human-annotated pairs; two outputs equivalent if 'a user who saw one would likely benefit from seeing the other'; 79% acc / 0.811 F1). distinct_k = number of unique classes. The headline metric utility_k = (1-p)/(1-p^k) * sum_i p^(i-1) * 1[c_i is new] * u_i, where p=0.8 is a user-patience discount, the indicator zeroes out redundant (duplicate-class) outputs, and u_i is a reward-model quality score (Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B) on a 1-10 scale. So a generation only earns credit if it is BOTH new and high-quality. Across 20 frontier models all show low diversity, and larger/more-capable models within a family are LESS diverse.
how_to_use: Use utility_k as the core scoreboard: it operationalizes 'distinct AND good' in one number by giving zero marginal credit to a duplicate no matter how high its reward score, and zero credit to a novel-but-low-quality output. Adopt their two-stage protocol verbatim — (1) cluster samples by a learned functional-equivalence model, (2) score each cluster representative with an independent reward model, (3) aggregate with a discounted novelty-weighted utility. This is the right shape for benchmarking whether a system makes a model 'think outside the box': measure new equivalence classes surfaced, not new wordings.
verified_title: NoveltyBench: Evaluating Language Models for Humanlike Diversity
verification_note: Exact title and full author list match the retrieved arXiv record.

title: Measuring LLM Novelty As The Frontier Of Original And High-Quality Output
authors: Vishakh Padmakumar, Chen Yueh-Han, Jane Pan, Valerie Chen, He He
finding: Novelty must be the joint frontier of originality and quality, because each alone is gameable: originality alone rewards low-quality gibberish, and human/judge quality alone rewards memorized (regurgitated) text. They define novelty as the HARMONIC MEAN of (a) originality = fraction of 4/5/6-grams in the output that never appear in the model's pre- and post-training corpora (measured against the actual training data via Infini-gram / WIMBD indexes), and (b) quality (LLM-judge, renormalized to 0-1). The harmonic mean punishes a low value on either axis. Key result: scaling and post-training raise novelty mainly via quality; inference-time tricks (temperature, novelty prompting, Denial Prompting, novel ICL) barely shift the frontier — temperature buys originality but loses quality.
how_to_use: Gives an honesty anchor against the single biggest trap — rewarding memorized regurgitation. To claim a system produces genuinely 'new' content, measure originality against the actual training corpus with an n-gram index (Infini-gram), not against a held-out reference set, and combine with quality via harmonic mean so neither axis can be traded away. Their negative result on inference-time methods is the benchmark baseline you must beat: any 'think-outside-the-box' system must move the originality-quality frontier, not just raise temperature.
verified_title: Measuring LLM Novelty As The Frontier Of Original And High-Quality Output
verification_note: Exact title and full author list match the retrieved arXiv record.

title: The Vendi Score: A Diversity Evaluation Metric for Machine Learning
authors: Dan Friedman, Adji Bousso Dieng
finding: A reference-free diversity metric = exponential of the Shannon entropy of the eigenvalues of an n-by-n similarity matrix (built from any user-chosen similarity kernel over the samples). It reports an 'effective number of distinct modes' — e.g. 10 outputs that collapse to 2 themes score ~2, not 10. Crucially it needs NO reference distribution and is monotone in true diversity, so it does not saturate the way distinct-n does and is not fooled by a few near-duplicates the way averaged self-BLEU can be. Quality-Weighted Vendi extensions (2405.02449) fold per-sample quality into the same eigenvalue computation.
how_to_use: Use Vendi (with an embedding-based similarity kernel) as the within-prompt diversity number that reports an interpretable effective-mode count, complementing NoveltyBench's discrete cluster count. Report Quality-Weighted Vendi so diversity credit is conditioned on quality. Because it is reference-free and similarity-function-agnostic, you can swap the kernel (semantic embedding vs. structural) to prove diversity isn't merely lexical reshuffling.
verified_title: The Vendi Score: A Diversity Evaluation Metric for Machine Learning
verification_note: Exact title and both authors match the retrieved arXiv record.

title: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
authors: Yann Dubois, Balázs Galambosi, Percy Liang, Tatsunori B. Hashimoto
finding: LLM-as-a-judge quality scores are systematically inflated by output length: longer answers win more regardless of added value, and this length bias is far stronger than in human raters. They fit a GLM predicting the judge's preference from mediators (notably length difference) and then evaluate the counterfactual 'what if both outputs were the same length?', which raises Spearman correlation with Chatbot Arena from 0.94 to 0.98 and makes the metric far harder to game by padding.
how_to_use: Directly defuses the verbosity trap in the quality half of any novelty score. Because a 'diverse' system can game judge-based quality (and even originality, via more n-grams) simply by writing longer, length-control every quality measurement: regress out length and report length-controlled scores, or hold token budget fixed across systems. Without this, a divergence benchmark can show fake gains that are pure verbosity.
verified_title: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
verification_note: Exact title and full author list match the retrieved arXiv record.

title: Texygen: A Benchmarking Platform for Text Generation Models (introduces Self-BLEU)
authors: Yaoming Zhu, Sidi Lu, Lei Zheng, Jiaxian Guo, Weinan Zhang, Jun Wang, Yong Yu
finding: Establishes the standard lexical diversity metrics — Self-BLEU (average BLEU of each generation against all others; lower = more diverse) alongside distinct-n — and pairs them with quality metrics (BLEU/NLL) so quality and diversity are reported jointly rather than in isolation. The platform's core methodological point: a model can win on quality while collapsing on diversity (and vice versa), so any single-axis score is misleading.
how_to_use: Use Self-BLEU and distinct-n only as cheap, transparent SURFACE-level sanity checks / lower bounds, never as the headline. They are useful precisely because they are easy to audit and hard to fake without real lexical change — but they reward paraphrase and miss semantic duplication, so they must sit underneath the semantic metrics (NoveltyBench clusters, Vendi). Report them to show the diversity gain isn't an artifact of one metric's blind spot.
verified_title: Texygen: A Benchmarking Platform for Text Generation Models
verification_note: Title (Texygen: A Benchmarking Platform for Text Generation Models) and full author list match the retrieved arXiv record; candidate title's parenthetical '(introduces Self-BLEU)' is an annotation, not part of the official title.

title: Justice or Prejudice? Quantifying Biases in LLM-as-a-Judge
authors: Jiayi Ye, Yanbo Wang, Yue Huang, Dongping Chen, Qihui Zhang, Nuno Moniz, Tian Gao, Werner Geyer, Chao Huang, Pin-Yu Chen, Nitesh V. Chawla, Xiangliang Zhang
finding: Systematically catalogs and quantifies 12 judge biases (verbosity, position/order, self-preference/egocentric, authority, sentiment, bandwagon, distraction, etc.) with an automated framework (CALM) that perturbs inputs to measure each bias without human labels. Shows even strong judges have measurable, exploitable biases — including self-preference, where a judge favors text from its own model family.
how_to_use: Defines the audit checklist for the quality half of a novelty benchmark. Avoid grading-your-own-homework (self-preference) by never using the same model family as generator and judge; randomize/swap positions to kill order bias; run CALM-style perturbation probes (e.g., inject authority cues, vary length) to confirm your judge's quality scores track substance, not style. Pair with length-control (2404.04475) to neutralize the strongest single bias.
verified_title: Justice or Prejudice? Quantifying Biases in LLM-as-a-Judge
verification_note: Exact title and full author list match the retrieved arXiv record.

title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
authors: Lianmin Zheng, Wei-Lin Chiang, Ying Sheng, Siyuan Zhuang, Zhanghao Wu, Yonghao Zhuang, Zi Lin, Zhuohan Li, Dacheng Li, Eric P. Xing, Hao Zhang, Joseph E. Gonzalez, Ion Stoica
finding: Foundational catalogue of LLM-judge failure modes: names and demonstrates position bias (judge favors first/second slot), verbosity bias (judge prefers longer answers even when content is padded, not better), and self-enhancement bias (a judge favors answers produced by itself or its own model family). It also shows a strong judge (GPT-4) can reach ~80%+ agreement with human preference — the same as human-human agreement — but ONLY after these biases are controlled (e.g. swapping positions and counting a win only if consistent both ways).
how_to_use: This is the bias taxonomy your novelty benchmark must defend against, item by item. Adopt its swap-and-require-consistency rule as the default decision unit: every novelty comparison is run in both A/B orders and a 'more novel' verdict only counts if it survives the swap; ties/flips are recorded as no-decision rather than silently broken. Use its ~80% human-agreement figure as the calibration target your judge must hit on a human-labeled novelty subset before any automated number is trusted.
verified_title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
authors: Peiyi Wang, Lei Li, Liang Chen, Zefan Cai, Dawei Zhu, Binghuai Lin, Yunbo Cao, Qi Liu, Tianyu Liu, Zhifang Sui
finding: Position bias is not noise — it is exploitable. Merely reordering two responses flips the verdict enough that a weaker model (Vicuna-13B) can be made to 'beat' a stronger one (ChatGPT) on 66/80 queries just by sitting in the favored slot. Proposes a concrete calibration framework: Multiple Evidence Calibration (judge writes reasoning/evidence BEFORE emitting a score), Balanced Position Calibration (average over both orderings), and Human-in-the-Loop Calibration using a balanced-position-diversity-entropy to flag hard cases for human review.
how_to_use: Gives the exact debiasing recipe to bake into the novelty judge: (1) force evidence-before-score so the judge can't post-hoc rationalize a slot preference, (2) average over both orderings rather than judging once, (3) compute a per-item disagreement-entropy and route high-entropy novelty pairs to human adjudication instead of reporting a fabricated automated winner. Items where the verdict depends on order are a measurement of judge unreliability, not of novelty — report that flip-rate as a headline honesty statistic.
verified_title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
authors: Yann Dubois, Balázs Galambosi, Percy Liang, Tatsunori B. Hashimoto
finding: Verbosity is a causal confound you can statistically remove. The auto-judge's preference is regressed on a length-difference term (plus other features) with a generalized linear model, then the length term is set to its counterfactual zero — answering 'what would the judge have preferred if both outputs were the same length?'. This makes the score un-gameable by simply asking a model to be more verbose, and raises correlation with human-grounded Chatbot Arena rankings from 0.94 to 0.98.
how_to_use: Directly transferable: novelty answers are often longer (more ideas, more elaboration), so a raw LLM-judge 'more novel' score is partly just 'more text'. Log token length for every output and fit the same length-controlled GLM so the reported novelty effect is the residual after length is partialled out. Critical adversarial test: run your own system's outputs at matched verbosity (and an inflated-verbosity variant) — if the novelty win shrinks or vanishes under length control, the original number was verbosity, not divergence.
verified_title: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment
authors: Yang Liu, Dan Iter, Yichong Xu, Shuohang Wang, Ruochen Xu, Chenguang Zhu
finding: Chain-of-thought + form-filling LLM scoring correlates with humans far better than lexical metrics (GPT-4 Spearman 0.514 on summarization, beating prior methods by a wide margin), AND raises the alarm that LLM evaluators are biased toward LLM-generated text. It explicitly warns that using such a judge as a reward/selection signal causes self-reinforcement: the system over-fits to the judge's own notion of quality rather than the true criterion.
how_to_use: Two uses. (1) Use its CoT form-filling rubric structure — define novelty as explicit sub-criteria (e.g. non-obviousness vs. the prompt's expected answer, semantic distance from the modal response, surprise) and have the judge fill each with justification, rather than emitting one opaque 'novelty: 8'. (2) Treat its self-reinforcement warning as a hard design constraint: if your system selects/optimizes outputs using an LLM judge, you cannot also evaluate final novelty with that same judge or family — that loop measures conformity to the judge, not real divergence. Use an independent judge model lineage for the final score.
verified_title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
authors: Koki Wataoka, Tsubasa Takahashi, Ryokan Ri
finding: Pins down the MECHANISM of self-preference: LLM judges assign systematically higher scores to LOW-PERPLEXITY (familiar-to-them) text than humans do, regardless of who actually wrote it. So the bias isn't really 'I recognize my own output' — it's 'I reward text that looks like my own distribution.' Provides a quantitative way to measure it via the judge's perplexity on the candidate.
how_to_use: This is the deepest trap for a NOVELTY benchmark specifically: genuinely divergent, surprising output is by definition HIGHER perplexity to the judge, so a familiarity-biased judge will systematically UNDER-score true novelty and OVER-score fluent, on-distribution paraphrase. Mitigations: (a) log the judge's perplexity/log-prob over each candidate and check the novelty score isn't just inversely tracking perplexity; (b) regress out a perplexity term as a confounder the way length is regressed in AlpacaEval; (c) never let the same model that generated a candidate judge it, and prefer a judge from a different family than the generator. Report judge-perplexity-vs-novelty-score correlation as an honesty metric.
verified_title: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
authors: Lin Shi, Chiyu Ma, Wenhua Liang, Xingjian Diao, Weicheng Ma, Soroush Vosoughi
finding: Large-scale (15 judges, ~40 generators, 22 tasks, 150k+ judgments) decomposition of position bias into three measurable quantities: Repetition Stability (same input judged twice → same verdict?), Position Consistency (verdict survives swapping order?), and Preference Fairness (net directional lean toward a slot). Key result: position bias is driven mainly by the QUALITY GAP between the two answers (close calls are where bias dominates) and only weakly by length — so bias concentrates exactly on the hard, ambiguous comparisons.
how_to_use: Gives you the three concrete reliability metrics to report alongside any novelty result. Before trusting the judge: measure Repetition Stability (run identical pairs at temp 0 and at temp>0 to expose flakiness), Position Consistency (the swap test), and Preference Fairness (does the judge net-favor slot A?). Because bias is worst when the two outputs are close in quality — which is precisely the regime of 'is this output genuinely MORE novel or just different?' — you must report the consistency rate specifically on near-tie novelty pairs, not just the easy ones, or the headline novelty number is borrowed from the easy cases.
verified_title: Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
verification_note: Title and full author list match exactly.

title: Evaluating LLMs' Divergent Thinking Capabilities for Scientific Idea Generation with Minimal Context (LiveIdeaBench)
authors: Kai Ruan, Xuan Wang, Jixiang Hong, Peng Wang, Yang Liu, Hao Sun
finding: A working blueprint for judging divergence specifically: scores ideas on separated dimensions (originality, feasibility, fluency, flexibility, clarity) using a DYNAMIC PANEL of multiple state-of-the-art LLM judges, with each idea scored by at least three randomly assigned critics from the panel to dilute any single judge's idiosyncratic bias. Approaches human-level on divergent-thinking assessment while flagging that judgment-rationale opacity remains the weak point.
how_to_use: Adopt its panel-of-independent-critics design as your anti-self-preference scaffold for the novelty axis: never a single judge, always ≥3 judges drawn from different model families (none of them the generator), with originality scored as its own dimension separate from feasibility/clarity so a polished-but-derivative answer can't win novelty on fluency. Use inter-judge agreement across the panel as a confidence gate, and require each critic to emit a rationale (addressing its opacity caveat) so novelty verdicts are auditable rather than trusted blindly.
verified_title: Evaluating LLMs' Divergent Thinking Capabilities for Scientific Idea Generation with Minimal Context
verification_note: Paper is real; arXiv title is 'Evaluating LLMs' Divergent Thinking Capabilities for Scientific Idea Generation with Minimal Context' (the 'LiveIdeaBench' label in the candidate title is the project name, not part of the arXiv title). Authors match exactly.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
authors: Mark Chen, Jerry Tworek, Heewoo Jun, Qiming Yuan, et al. (OpenAI)
finding: Introduces the pass@k metric AND its unbiased estimator. The naive estimate (generate k, check if any pass) is high-variance and biased; the correct estimator generates n >= k samples, counts c correct, and computes pass@k = E[1 - C(n-c, k)/C(n, k)]. They show single-sample success is modest (28.8%) but repeated sampling rescues many problems (70.2% at k=100), establishing that pass@k measures something fundamentally different from pass@1.
how_to_use: Use the unbiased combinatorial estimator, not the naive 'did any of k pass' count, or you will overstate the divergent engine's coverage with high variance. Sample n >> k (e.g. n=200 to report pass@k for k up to ~50) and report the estimator with bootstrap confidence intervals over problems. Pass@1 and pass@k are different axes: a divergence engine should be judged on whether it lifts the pass@k *frontier*, not just pass@1.
verified_title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title matches exactly and author list begins Mark Chen, Jerry Tworek, Heewoo Jun, Qiming Yuan (OpenAI Codex paper).

title: Large Language Monkeys: Scaling Inference Compute with Repeated Sampling
authors: Bradley Brown, Jordan Juravsky, Ryan Ehrlich, Ronald Clark, Quoc V. Le, Christopher Ré, Azalia Mirhoseini
finding: Coverage (fraction of problems solved by ANY of k samples) scales log-linearly with k across four orders of magnitude, modelable as an exponentiated power law. Critically: coverage requires a *verifier* to convert into usable accuracy, and verifier-free selection (majority vote, reward models) PLATEAUS after a few hundred samples while coverage keeps climbing. So 'coverage' is an upper bound that only materializes if you can pick the winner.
how_to_use: Separate two numbers in the benchmark: (a) coverage/pass@k = does the engine ever produce the novel-correct answer, and (b) selected accuracy = can the engine PICK it without an oracle. Reporting (a) alone is the single biggest way to flatter a multi-sample engine. Plot both vs log(samples) and vs FLOPs. Only claim 'thinks outside the box usefully' if selected accuracy (with a realistic, non-oracle verifier) beats the baseline at matched compute — not just coverage.
verified_title: Large Language Monkeys: Scaling Inference Compute with Repeated Sampling
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title and full author list match exactly.

title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters
authors: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, Aviral Kumar
finding: The right way to compare test-time-compute methods is a FLOPs-matched (compute-matched) evaluation. Under matched budget, the best strategy depends on prompt difficulty: a 'compute-optimal' adaptive allocation beats best-of-N by >4x efficiency, and test-time compute can beat a 14x larger model ONLY on problems where the base model already has non-trivial success. On the hardest problems extra sampling does not close the gap.
how_to_use: Define the X-axis as total compute (FLOPs or wall-clock or $), not number of samples — a multi-sample engine that runs N rollouts, a search tree, plus a verifier must be charged for ALL of it and compared against the single-pass baseline given the SAME budget (e.g. baseline gets best-of-N at equal FLOPs, or a larger model at equal FLOPs). Stratify results by problem difficulty bin; report where divergence helps and where it is wasted compute, rather than a single aggregate.
verified_title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title and authors (Snell, Lee, Xu, Kumar) match exactly.

title: Does Reinforcement Learning Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model?
authors: Yang Yue, Zhiqi Chen, Rui Lu, Andrew Zhao, Zhaokai Wang, Yang Yue, Shiji Song, Gao Huang (Tsinghua LeapLab)
finding: Measuring at LARGE k flips conclusions. RLVR-trained models beat their base model at pass@1 / small k, but the BASE model achieves higher pass@k at large k — i.e. RL sharpens selection of already-reachable solutions and actually NARROWS the support of the sampling distribution rather than expanding the reasoning boundary. The 'boundary' is only visible if you push k high enough; small-k metrics give the opposite story.
how_to_use: This is the core honest test for 'thinking outside the box': does the engine EXPAND the set of reachable correct solutions (raise pass@k at large k / coverage), or merely re-weight toward solutions the baseline could already sample? Always report the full pass@k curve out to large k (hundreds–thousands), not just pass@1. If the divergent engine wins at k=1 but the baseline catches/overtakes at large k, the engine is improving selection/presentation, NOT generating genuinely novel capability — and you must say so.
verified_title: Does Reinforcement Learning Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model?
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title matches exactly. arXiv author list: Yang Yue, Zhiqi Chen, Rui Lu, Andrew Zhao, Zhaokai Wang, Shiji Song, Gao Huang (candidate's duplicate 'Yang Yue' is a minor artifact; otherwise matches).

title: Keep Guessing? When Considering Inference Scaling, Mind the Baselines
authors: Gal Yona, Or Honovich, Omer Levy, Roee Aharoni
finding: A large part of repeated-sampling coverage gains is explained by benchmark answer distributions being skewed toward a few common answers. A trivial baseline that just ENUMERATES answers by their training-set prevalence matches or beats repeated model sampling for several LLMs; a mixture (10 real model samples + guess the rest by frequency) is on par with pure sampling. So rising coverage can be 'guessing popular answers,' not reasoning.
how_to_use: Include a 'dumb-guessing' control: enumerate the k most frequent answers (or k random plausible answers) and compute its pass@k. The divergence engine must beat this prior-enumeration baseline, otherwise its 'coverage' is exploiting a skewed answer space, not producing novel solutions. Prefer open-ended / free-form tasks with large answer spaces (proofs, code, designs) over multiple-choice or small-integer-answer math where guessing dominates.
verified_title: Keep Guessing? When Considering Inference Scaling, Mind the Baselines
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title and authors (Yona, Honovich, Levy, Aharoni) match exactly.

title: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
authors: Koki Wataoka, Tsubasa Takahashi, Ryokan Ri
finding: LLM judges systematically overrate outputs that are familiar to them (lower perplexity), and the field-wide pattern is a strong length/verbosity bias far exceeding human raters — judges prefer longer answers and their own-style outputs. Self-preference is quantifiable and significant (e.g. GPT-4). This directly corrupts any 'novelty/quality' score produced by an LLM judge.
how_to_use: If you grade 'divergence/novelty' with an LLM judge, you are measuring length and self-style, not insight. Mitigations to bake into the protocol: use a verifiable ground-truth signal (unit tests, formal checker, exact-match) wherever possible; if a judge is unavoidable, control for length (length-matched or length-controlled win rate), use a judge from a DIFFERENT model family than the system under test (never grade your own homework), blind/randomize answer order, and validate judge scores against a human-labeled subset.
verified_title: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
verification_note: Retrieved this call; title and authors (Wataoka, Takahashi, Ri) match exactly.
